meta.subject: Indian Polity & Governance
meta.primary_book: Indian Polity by M. Laxmikanth (6th Edition) — 80+ Chapters
ncert_books: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work (Political Science Part I)
Class 11 — Political Theory (Political Science Part II)
Class 12 — Politics in India Since Independence (Political Science Part I)
Class 12 — Contemporary World Politics (Political Science Part II)
supplementary: DD Basu — Introduction to the Constitution of India
Vision IAS Polity Notes (Current Affairs Integration)
meta.reading_order: NCERT Class 9 → NCERT Class 10 → NCERT Class 11 Part I → Laxmikanth → NCERT Class 12 Part I → Current Affairs

ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 2: Constitutional Design
Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 1: Constitution — Why and How?
key_concepts: Government of India Act 1858 — transfer from Company to Crown
Indian Councils Act 1861, 1892, 1909 (Morley-Minto Reforms)
Government of India Act 1919 (Montague-Chelmsford Reforms) — Dyarchy
Government of India Act 1935 — Federation, Provincial Autonomy, Dyarchy at Centre
Indian Independence Act 1947 — Partition, Dominion Status
Regulating Act 1773 — first step of British Parliamentary control
Pitts India Act 1784
Charter Acts 1813, 1833, 1853

ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 2: Constitutional Design
Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 1: Constitution — Why and How?
key_concepts: Constituent Assembly — formation, composition, 389 members
Demand for Constituent Assembly — M.N. Roy 1934, INC 1935, Cripps Mission 1942
Cabinet Mission Plan 1946 — elections to Constituent Assembly
Committees of Constituent Assembly — Drafting Committee (B.R. Ambedkar)
Sessions of Constituent Assembly — 11 sessions, 2 years 11 months 17 days
Enactment — Nov 26 1949 (adopted), Jan 26 1950 (enforced)
Key personalities — Rajendra Prasad (President), B.R. Ambedkar (Chairman Drafting)
Jawaharlal Nehru — Objectives Resolution Dec 1946

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 1
key_concepts: Lengthiest written constitution in the world — 395 articles (originally), 22 parts, 8 schedules
Current: 470+ articles, 25 parts, 12 schedules, 5 appendices
Drawn from various sources — borrowed features chart
Partly rigid, partly flexible
Federal with unitary bias
Parliamentary form of government
Integrated and independent judiciary
Fundamental Rights — justiciable
DPSP — non-justiciable
Secular state
Universal Adult Franchise
Single citizenship
Emergency provisions
Three-tier government (after 73rd & 74th Amendments)

sources_borrowed.UK: Parliamentary system, Rule of Law, single citizenship, Cabinet system, prerogative writs, parliamentary privileges, bicameralism
sources_borrowed.USA: Fundamental Rights, judicial review, independence of judiciary, removal of SC/HC judges, preamble, Vice-President's role
sources_borrowed.Ireland: DPSP, method of election of President, nomination of members to Rajya Sabha
sources_borrowed.Canada: Federal system with strong centre, residuary powers with centre, advisory jurisdiction of SC
sources_borrowed.Australia: Concurrent List, trade/commerce/intercourse provisions, joint sitting of Parliament
sources_borrowed.South Africa: Amendment procedure of Constitution, election of members of Rajya Sabha
sources_borrowed.USSR: Fundamental Duties, ideal of justice (social, economic, political) in Preamble
sources_borrowed.France: Republic, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity in Preamble

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 1
key_concepts: Preamble text — memorise word for word
Preamble as key to Constitution — Berubari Case 1960
Preamble as part of Constitution — Kesavananda Bharati Case 1973
Nature of Indian State — Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic, Republic
Socialist added by 42nd Amendment 1976
Secular added by 42nd Amendment 1976
Objectives — Justice (social, economic, political), Liberty, Equality, Fraternity
Date of adoption — November 26, 1949
Article 1 — India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States
Difference between Federation and Union
Articles 1-4 — Territory of India
Types of territories — States, UTs, other territories
Parliament's power to form new states, alter boundaries — Article 3
States Reorganisation Act 1956 — linguistic basis
Recent statehood — Telangana 2014, J&K bifurcation 2019
UTs — current list and how formed

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Political Theory — Chapter 6: Citizenship
key_concepts: Citizenship Act 1955 — acquisition and termination
Citizenship by birth, descent, registration, naturalisation, incorporation of territory
Citizenship Amendment Act 2019 — Hindus, Sikhs, Jains, Buddhists, Parsis, Christians from Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Pakistan
Single citizenship in India
OCI — Overseas Citizenship of India (not dual citizenship)
PIO card vs OCI card
Articles 5-11 — Citizenship at commencement
Rights exclusive to citizens — FRs under Articles 15, 16, 19, 29, 30

revision_day_tasks: PYQ solving from 2015-2023 on this module

topics.topic_name: Fundamental Rights — Overview & Right to Equality
ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 5: Democratic Rights
Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 2: Rights in the Indian Constitution
key_concepts: Article 12 — Definition of State (includes government, Parliament, state legislature, local bodies, other authorities)
Article 13 — Laws inconsistent with FRs are void — basis of judicial review
Article 14 — Equality before law vs Equal protection of laws — difference
Article 15 — Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth
Article 15(3) — special provision for women & children
Article 15(4),(5) — reservations for SC/ST/OBCs — added by amendments
Article 16 — Equality of opportunity in public employment
Article 16(4) — reservations for backward classes
Article 17 — Abolition of Untouchability — protected civil rights act 1955
Article 18 — Abolition of Titles — exceptions for military/academic
Doctrine of Reasonable Classification — intelligible differentia + nexus test
important_cases: State of West Bengal v. Anwar Ali Sarkar (1952) — Article 14
Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (1992) — Mandal Commission, 50% cap on reservations

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 2
key_concepts: Article 19 — Six freedoms (originally 7, Article 19(1)(f) deleted by 44th Amendment)
19(1)(a) — Freedom of speech & expression + reasonable restrictions
19(1)(b) — Right to assemble peaceably without arms
19(1)(c) — Right to form associations/unions
19(1)(d) — Freedom of movement throughout territory of India
19(1)(e) — Right to reside and settle
19(1)(g) — Right to practise any profession/trade/business
Reasonable Restrictions under 19(2) to 19(6) — grounds listed
Article 20 — Protection in respect of conviction for offences (3 clauses)
Article 20(1) — No ex post facto law
Article 20(2) — No double jeopardy
Article 20(3) — No self-incrimination
Article 21 — Right to Life and Personal Liberty — most litigated article
Article 21A — Right to Education (86th Amendment, 2002) — 6-14 years
Article 22 — Protection against arrest and detention — 4 safeguards
Preventive detention — 3 months max without Advisory Board approval
important_cases: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) — expanded Article 21
Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) — Section 66A struck down
K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017) — Right to Privacy under Article 21
Hussainara Khatoon (1979) — Right to speedy trial under Article 21

topics.topic_name: Right Against Exploitation, Religion, Culture & Education
ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 2
key_concepts: Article 23 — Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
Article 24 — Prohibition of employment of children below 14 in factories
Article 25 — Freedom of conscience and free profession of religion
Article 26 — Freedom to manage religious affairs
Article 27 — No taxes for promotion of religion
Article 28 — No religious instruction in state-funded institutions
Article 29 — Protection of interests of minorities (language, script, culture)
Article 30 — Right of minorities to establish & administer educational institutions
Article 30 vs Article 29 — difference
Triple Talaq judgment — Shayara Bano v. Union of India 2017

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 2
key_concepts: Article 32 — Right to move SC for enforcement of FRs — 'Heart and soul of Constitution' (Ambedkar)
Article 32 itself a Fundamental Right
Article 226 — High Courts also issue writs but wider jurisdiction than Article 32
Five Writs — Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto
Habeas Corpus — 'you shall have the body' — against illegal detention
Mandamus — 'we command' — to perform public duty
Prohibition — issued by higher court to lower court to stop proceedings
Certiorari — to quash order of lower court
Quo Warranto — 'by what authority' — against illegal holding of public office
Article 33 — Parliament's power to restrict FRs for armed forces
Article 34 — Restriction during Martial Law
Article 35 — Parliament's power to make laws on certain FRs
important_cases: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (1976) — overruled by Puttaswamy 2017
Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950) — FR cannot be abridged

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 3: Election and Representation
key_concepts: Articles 36-51 — Part IV of Constitution
Non-justiciable — cannot be enforced in court
Borrowed from Irish Constitution
Classification — Socialistic, Gandhian, Liberal-Intellectual
Socialistic DPSPs — Articles 38, 39, 39A, 41, 42, 43, 43A, 47
Gandhian DPSPs — Articles 40, 43, 43B, 46, 47, 48
Liberal-Intellectual DPSPs — Articles 44, 45, 48A, 49, 50, 51
Key DPSPs — Article 44 (Uniform Civil Code), Article 45 (free education), Article 48A (environment)
FR vs DPSP conflict — Kesavananda Bharati (1973) — Harmonious Construction
42nd Amendment — DPSPs given precedence over Articles 14, 19 (later reversed)
Minerva Mills Case (1980) — balance between FR and DPSP must be maintained
Article 51A — Part IVA of Constitution
Added by 42nd Amendment 1976 — Swaran Singh Committee recommendation
Original 10 duties + 11th added by 86th Amendment 2002
11th duty — parent/guardian to provide education to child 6-14 years
Non-justiciable — no legal sanction
Borrowed from erstwhile USSR Constitution
Verma Committee (1999) — recommended legal sanctions for violation
Relevance — courts can use FDs to determine constitutionality of laws

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 9: Constitution as a Living Document
key_concepts: Article 368 — Power of Parliament to amend
Three methods — Simple majority, Special majority, Special majority + State ratification
Simple majority — Articles outside Article 368 (e.g., new states, citizenship)
Special majority — 2/3rd of members present and voting + majority of total membership
Special majority + 50% states ratification — federal provisions (Article 368(2))
Key Amendments — 1st, 7th, 24th, 25th, 42nd, 44th, 52nd, 61st, 73rd, 74th, 86th, 91st, 97th, 99th, 100th, 101st (GST), 102nd, 103rd (EWS)
Basic Structure Doctrine — Kesavananda Bharati (1973) — Parliament cannot destroy basic structure
Elements of basic structure — supremacy of Constitution, rule of law, judicial review, secular character, etc.

topics.laxmikanth_chapter: Chapter 11 — Basic Structure of the Constitution
key_concepts: Golak Nath Case 1967 — Parliament cannot amend FRs
24th Amendment 1971 — overruled Golak Nath — gave Parliament power to amend any part
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala 1973 — 13-judge bench, 7:6 majority
Basic Structure = Parliament can amend but cannot destroy/abrogate
Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain 1975 — free and fair elections part of basic structure
Minerva Mills 1980 — Articles 14, 19 part of basic structure
S.R. Bommai 1994 — federalism part of basic structure

Elements of basic structure (not exhaustive) — supremacy of Constitution, sovereignty, democratic republic, secular state, separation of powers, judicial review, FR under Article 32, free & fair elections, rule of law, parliamentary system

ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 4: Working of Institutions
Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 4: Executive
key_concepts: Parliamentary vs Presidential system — key differences

Features of Parliamentary system in India — nominal & real executive, majority party rule, collective responsibility, political homogeneity, double membership, leadership of PM, dissolution of lower house, no fixed tenure
Merits and demerits of Parliamentary system
Why India chose Parliamentary system — familiarity with Westminster model, heterogeneous society needs consensus

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 4: Executive
key_concepts: Articles 52-62, 71-73
Election — Electoral College (elected MPs + elected MLAs), single transferable vote, proportional representation
Qualifications — Article 58
Term — 5 years (Article 56), re-eligible
Impeachment — Article 61, both Houses, 2/3rd majority of total membership
Executive Powers — Article 53, 74, 75, 77, 78
Legislative Powers — summon/prorogue/dissolve, address, send messages, Table Bills, assent to Bills
Discretionary Powers — appointment of PM when no clear majority, dissolve Lok Sabha when PM loses confidence
Veto Powers — Absolute, Suspensive, Pocket Veto (no time limit for assent to ordinary bills)
Ordinance-making power — Article 123 (same force as Parliamentary law, when Parliament not in session)
Emergency Powers — National (352), President's Rule (356), Financial (360)
Pardoning Powers — Article 72 (Pardon, Reprieve, Respite, Remission, Commutation) — includes death sentence
President vs Governor — Article 72 vs Article 161 (Governor cannot pardon death sentence)
Articles 63-71
Election — Electoral College (elected + nominated members of both Houses), no MLAs
Ex-officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha
Acts as President during vacancy — maximum 6 months
Removal — Rajya Sabha absolute majority + Lok Sabha concurrence
No specific grounds needed for removal — unlike President (impeachment)

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 4: Executive
key_concepts: Article 74 — Council of Ministers to aid and advise President
Article 75 — PM appointed by President, other ministers on PM's advice
Collective responsibility — Article 75(3) — Council of Ministers responsible to Lok Sabha
Individual responsibility — Article 75(2) — minister holds office during President's pleasure
Cabinet, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Committees — distinctions
PM's role — leader of Cabinet, coordinate ministries, appoints ministers
PM's special powers — recommends dissolution of Lok Sabha to President
91st Amendment 2003 — 15% cap on size of Council of Ministers
Cabinet Committees — not mentioned in Constitution — extra-constitutional
Kitchen Cabinet / Inner Cabinet — informal concept

ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 4
Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 5: Legislature
key_concepts: Lok Sabha — Article 81, max 552 members (530 states + 20 UTs + 2 Anglo-Indian — now omitted by 104th Amendment)
Current Lok Sabha — 543 seats
Rajya Sabha — Article 80, max 250 (238 elected + 12 nominated)
Current Rajya Sabha — 245
Rajya Sabha — permanent house, not subject to dissolution, 1/3rd retire every 2 years
Sessions — Budget, Monsoon, Winter
Summoning — President on advice of PM (Article 85)
Prorogation vs Dissolution vs Adjournment
Quorum — 1/10th of total membership
Presiding officers — Speaker (LS), Chairman (RS), Deputy Speaker, Deputy Chairman
Speaker removal — 14 days notice + absolute majority resolution
Speaker's casting vote in case of tie
Parliamentary procedures — Question Hour, Zero Hour, Calling Attention Motion
Bills — Ordinary, Money, Finance, Constitutional Amendment
Money Bill — Article 110, Speaker certifies, only Lok Sabha introduces, RS cannot amend only recommend, 14 days

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 5
key_concepts: Standing Committees vs Ad Hoc Committees
Financial Committees — Public Accounts Committee (PAC), Estimates Committee, Committee on Public Undertakings
PAC — oldest committee, examines CAG report, chaired by Opposition leader
Departmentally Related Standing Committees (DRSCs) — 24 committees
Other Committees — Business Advisory, Rules, Privileges, Ethics
Joint Parliamentary Committee (JPC) — ad hoc, specific purpose
Parliamentary Forums — informal bodies for MPs
Motions — Censure vs No-Confidence (difference), Adjournment, Calling Attention, Cut Motion (Policy Cut, Economy Cut, Token Cut)
Whip — party whip, three-line whip, role in Anti-Defection Law
Privilege Motion — breach of parliamentary privilege
Points of Order — raised during proceedings

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 6: Judiciary
key_concepts: Articles 124-147
Composition — CJI + 33 judges (increased from original 7)
Appointment — collegium system (not in Constitution) — CJI + 4 senior-most judges
Second Judges Case 1993, Third Judges Case 1998 — collegium established
NJAC — 99th Amendment 2014 struck down by SC 2015 (Supreme Court Advocates on Record Association case)
Removal — Article 124(4) — impeachment, address of both Houses, special majority, proven misbehaviour/incapacity
Independence of SC — security of tenure, fixed service conditions, charged on Consolidated Fund, no practice after retirement
Jurisdiction — Original (Article 131), Appellate (Article 132-136), Advisory (Article 143), Writ (Article 32), Review
Original jurisdiction — disputes between states, between state and centre
Special Leave Petition (SLP) — Article 136 — SC can grant leave to appeal any judgment
Curative Petition — after review dismissed
Presidential Reference — Article 143 — advisory opinion, not binding
Judicial Review — Article 13, 32 — power to declare laws unconstitutional
Judicial Activism vs Judicial Overreach
PIL — Public Interest Litigation — Locus Standi relaxed
10th Schedule — added by 52nd Amendment 1985
Defection — voluntarily giving up party membership OR voting against party direction
Exception — merger (2/3rd members agree)
Earlier exception of split (1/3rd) removed by 91st Amendment 2003
Decision by Speaker/Chairman — subject to judicial review (Kihoto Hollohan 1992)
Nabam Rebia Case 2016 — Speaker himself facing removal cannot decide defection
Criticism — goes against conscience voting, stifles dissent

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 7: Federalism
key_concepts: India — 'Union of States' not 'Federation of States' (Article 1)
Features of federalism — dual government, written Constitution, supremacy of Constitution, rigidity, independent judiciary, bicameralism
Unitary bias features — strong centre, single Constitution, single citizenship, integrated judiciary, appointed governors, all-India services, emergency provisions
Quasi-federal — KC Wheare's description
Cooperative federalism vs Competitive federalism
Sarkaria Commission 1983 — Centre-State relations recommendations
Punchhi Commission 2007 — second commission on Centre-State relations
M.M. Punchhi Committee recommendations — Governor's role, Article 356

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 7
key_concepts: Three Lists — Union (List I — 100 subjects), State (List II — 61 subjects), Concurrent (List III — 52 subjects)
Residuary powers — with Centre (Article 248) — unlike USA where residuary with states
Parliament's power to legislate on State List — Articles 249, 250, 252, 253
Article 249 — RS passes resolution 2/3rd + state of national interest
Article 250 — during National Emergency
Article 252 — 2 or more states request Parliament
Article 253 — to implement international treaties
Administrative Relations — Articles 256-263
Article 256 — states must comply with Parliament laws
Article 257 — states not to impede Union executive power
All India Services — Article 312 — IAS, IPS, IFS (bridges administrative gap)
Financial Relations — Articles 268-293
Finance Commission — Article 280 — devolution of taxes
15th Finance Commission — 41% share to states (from 42%)
GST Council — Article 279A — 101st Amendment
Grants-in-aid — Articles 275, 282
Articles 261-263
Interstate Councils — Article 263 — coordinate between states (not same as Zonal Councils)
Zonal Councils — statutory bodies, 5 zones, set up under States Reorganisation Act 1956
Interstate Water Disputes — Article 262 — Parliament excludes SC jurisdiction
Full Faith and Credit Clause — Article 261
River Water Disputes Tribunals — Krishna, Cauvery, Godavari

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 8: Local Governments
key_concepts: Three Emergencies — National (352), President's Rule (356), Financial (360)
National Emergency — war, external aggression, armed rebellion (44th Amendment — 'internal disturbance' replaced)
Proclamation — Cabinet recommendation in writing, President proclaims
Approval — both Houses within 1 month, 2/3rd special majority
Duration — 6 months, renewable indefinitely with approval every 6 months
Effect — Centre can legislate on State List, executive power extends to states
FRs — Article 358 (19 suspended automatically), Article 359 (others suspended by President)
President's Rule — Article 356 — failure of constitutional machinery
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India 1994 — Floor test must be held before dismissal of state govt
44th Amendment — Article 356 approval required within 2 months, limit 3 years
Financial Emergency — Article 360 — never been proclaimed
44th Amendment 1978 — major safeguards against misuse of Article 352

topics.laxmikanth_chapter: Chapters 30-33 — Governor, CM, State CoM, State Legislature
ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 4
key_concepts: Governor — Articles 153-162
Appointment — by President, not elected
Qualifications — Article 157 (citizen, 35+ years)
Term — 5 years (at pleasure of President)
Dual role — constitutional head of state + agent of Centre
Discretionary powers — appointment of CM (hung assembly), refusal of assent, reserving bills for President, dismissal of ministry
Pocket Veto — Governor unlike President has time limit (Article 200 — 'as soon as possible')
Chief Minister — Articles 163-167
State Council of Ministers — collective responsibility to State Legislature
State Legislature — unicameral (22 states) or bicameral (6 states — AP, Bihar, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Telangana, UP)
State Legislative Council (Vidhan Parishad) — 1/3rd elected by MLAs, 1/3rd local bodies, 1/12th teachers, 1/12th graduates, 1/6th nominated by Governor
Session, Quorum, State Bills procedure

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 6
key_concepts: Articles 214-231 — High Courts
Appointment — collegium + Governor's consultation
Jurisdiction — Original, Appellate, Writ (Article 226 — broader than SC)
Article 226 — HC writ jurisdiction extends to enforcement of any legal right (not just FRs)
Transfer of HC judges — by President after consultation with CJI
District Courts — Sessions Judge = District Judge
Gram Nyayalayas Act 2008 — mobile courts at grassroots
Lok Adalat — Legal Services Authority Act 1987 — no court fee, binding award
Fast Track Courts, Family Courts, Consumer Forums

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 8: Local Governments
key_concepts: 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act 1992 — effective April 24, 1993
Part IX added — Articles 243-243O
11th Schedule — 29 subjects transferred to Panchayats
Three-tier structure — Gram Panchayat, Panchayat Samiti, Zila Parishad
Gram Sabha — foundation of PR system
Duration — 5 years, elections before expiry
Reservation — 1/3rd for women (some states 50%), SC/ST proportional to population
State Finance Commission — review of financial position of PRIs
District Planning Committee — Article 243ZD
Balwant Rai Mehta Committee 1957 — recommended 3-tier PR (first)
Ashok Mehta Committee 1977 — recommended 2-tier
GVK Rao Committee, LM Singhvi Committee

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 8
key_concepts: 74th Constitutional Amendment Act 1992 — effective June 1, 1993
Part IX-A added — Articles 243P-243ZG
12th Schedule — 18 subjects transferred to Urban Local Bodies
Types of ULBs — Nagar Panchayat, Municipal Council, Municipal Corporation
Ward Committees for populations above 3 lakhs
Reservation — same as PRIs
Metropolitan Planning Committee — Article 243ZE
Smart Cities Mission — urban renewal
Article 239 — Administration of UTs by President through Administrator
Delhi — NCT — Article 239AA (69th Amendment) — special status, elected legislature, LG vs CM conflict
GNCT of Delhi Amendment Act 2021 — LG's opinion must be sought for certain matters
SC ruling 2023 — Delhi government has control over services (except police, land, public order)
Puducherry — elected legislature — Article 239A
J&K Reorganisation Act 2019 — J&K + Ladakh become UTs
Fifth Schedule — Scheduled Areas (tribal areas — non-northeast)
Sixth Schedule — Tribal areas of northeast (Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, Mizoram) — Autonomous District Councils
Article 323A — Administrative Tribunals — Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT)
Article 323B — other tribunals (Income Tax, Armed Forces, etc.)
L Chandra Kumar Case 1997 — tribunal decisions subject to HC/SC jurisdiction
97th Amendment 2011 — Part IXB — Co-operative Societies (Amendments later struck down partially by SC)
Special Provisions — Article 371 to 371J — special status for various states
Article 371A — Nagaland (customary law protected)
Article 371G — Mizoram (customary law protected)

ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 3: Electoral Politics
Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 3
key_concepts: Article 324 — ECI — superintendence, direction, control of elections
Composition — CEC + Election Commissioners (currently 3)
Appointment — by President
Removal — CEC removed like SC judge (Article 324(5)), ECs on CEC's recommendation
Election Laws Amendment Act 2023 — changed appointment (now committee of PM, Home Minister, Leader of Opposition)
Functions — schedule elections, model code of conduct, derecognise parties, EVM oversight
Model Code of Conduct — no legal backing, convention
NOTA — introduced 2013
Rajya Sabha elections — open ballot
Electoral Bond Scheme — struck down by SC 2024
Article 315-323 — UPSC
Composition — Chairman + 10 members (1/2 must have 10 years in govt service)
Appointment — by President, removal — by President after SC inquiry
UPSC functions — recruitment, promotions, transfers, disciplinary matters advice
UPSC not consulted for — emergency appointments, posts of Chairman of PSCs, SC/HC judges
Annual report — to President — laid before Parliament
State PSC — Article 315 — similar structure, appointment by Governor, removal by President

ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 5
key_concepts: CAG — Article 148 — audit of Union and State accounts
CAG appointment — by President, removal like SC judge
CAG reports — laid before Parliament — examined by PAC
Finance Commission — Article 280 — quasi-judicial body
FC set up every 5 years — recommends tax devolution, grants
15th Finance Commission (2021-26) — 41% to states
Attorney General — Article 76 — first law officer of India
Appointment — by President, qualifications same as SC judge
AG can practice in courts, vote in Parliament (no right to vote)
Solicitor General, Additional Solicitor General — below AG, not constitutional

topics.topic_name: National Commissions — SC, ST, BC, Minorities
key_concepts: National Commission for SCs — Article 338 — constitutional body
National Commission for STs — Article 338A — constitutional (89th Amendment 2003 — separated from NCSC)
National Commission for BCs — Article 338B — added by 102nd Amendment 2018
National Commission for Minorities — statutory (not constitutional) — Minorities Commission Act 1992
6 notified minorities — Muslims, Christians, Sikhs, Buddhists, Parsis, Jains (added 2014)
Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities — Article 350B
NHRC — Protection of Human Rights Act 1993 — statutory body
NHRC Chairperson — retired CJI (amended 2019 — any retired SC judge)
CIC — Central Information Commission — RTI Act 2005
CIC — Chief IC + 10 ICs — appointed by President on PM's committee recommendation
CVC — Central Vigilance Commission — statutory (CVC Act 2003)
CVC — single member body (CVC + 2 VCs)
CVC jurisdiction — Group A officers, PSU executives
Lokpal — Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013 (effective 2019)
Lokpal — Chairman (retired CJI or SC judge) + 8 members (50% judicial, 50% non-judicial, 50% SC/ST/OBC/minorities/women)
Lokpal jurisdiction — PM (with safeguards), ministers, MPs, Group A-D officials
PM excluded from jurisdiction for — national security, public order, international relations

topics.topic_name: NITI Aayog, NIA, NDMA & Other Bodies
key_concepts: NITI Aayog — replaced Planning Commission on Jan 1, 2015
NITI Aayog — Chairperson = PM, CEO appointed by PM, Governing Council = all CMs + LGs
Key differences — Planning Commission vs NITI Aayog (no fund allocation power)
NIA — National Investigation Agency — NIA Act 2008 (post 26/11)
NIA — federal agency, investigates scheduled offences, jurisdiction across states
NDMA — National Disaster Management Authority — DM Act 2005
NDMA Chairperson — PM
State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA) — CM is Chairperson
NDRF — National Disaster Response Force — under NDMA

topics.topic_name: Official Language, Public Services & Special Provisions
key_concepts: Official Language — Part XVII — Articles 343-351
Hindi in Devanagari script — Official language of Union
English continues — Official Languages Act 1963
8th Schedule — 22 languages (originally 14)
Classical language status — Sanskrit, Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, Malayalam, Odia
Article 351 — promote Hindi
Public Services — Article 308-314 — All India Services, Central Services
IAS, IPS, IFoS — All India Services (Article 312)
Article 311 — protection to civil servants (inquiry before dismissal)

ncert_reference: Class 10 — Democratic Politics II — Chapter 6: Political Parties
Class 12 — Politics in India Since Independence — Chapter 9
key_concepts: Recognition of parties — national vs state parties
ECI criteria for recognition — vote share and seat criteria
National parties — 6 currently (BJP, INC, BSP, CPI, CPI(M), NPP + AAP recently)
Election Symbol — reserved vs free symbols
Functions of political parties
Rise of regional parties — causes and significance
Coalition politics — NDA, INDIA Alliance, Third Front
Role of regional parties — stabilising or destabilising?

ncert_reference: Class 9 — Democratic Politics I — Chapter 3: Electoral Politics
Class 10 — Democratic Politics II — Chapter 3: Democracy and Diversity
key_concepts: First Past the Post (FPTP) system — for Lok Sabha, Vidhan Sabha
Proportional Representation — for President, VP, Rajya Sabha, Vidhan Parishad
Delimitation — Delimitation Act, Delimitation Commission
Representation of People Act 1950, 1951
Model Code of Conduct — binding or advisory?
Electoral Reforms — Law Commission recommendations
EVM controversy — VVPAT
One Nation One Election — pros and cons
Electoral Bonds — struck down by SC (Association for Democratic Reforms case 2024)
Campaign finance — Declaration of criminal antecedents, asset disclosure
Paid news — ECI's stand
Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail (VVPAT)

topics.topic_name: Comparison of Indian Constitution with Other Constitutions
ncert_reference: Class 11 — Indian Constitution at Work — Chapter 1
key_concepts: India vs USA — Presidential vs Parliamentary, judicial review origin, Bill of Rights vs FRs, written vs unwritten
India vs UK — India has written Constitution, India federal while UK unitary, India has judicial review UK lacks
India vs Canada — both federal but India stronger centre, Canada — residuary with provinces, India with Centre
India vs Australia — concurrent list borrowed from Australia
India vs South Africa — amendment procedure similarity, proportional representation for Rajya Sabha
India vs France — Preamble ideals (Liberty, Equality, Fraternity)
Separation of powers — India (partial) vs USA (strict)
Judicial Independence — India vs USA — appointment process comparison
Fundamental Rights — India vs USA Bill of Rights — key differences
